clinical_history: This ECG from a 78-year-old woman in critical condition shows all of the following EXCEPT?
diagnosis: The ECG shows sinus tachycardia (P wave rate of 100/min) with Mobitz I AV block (3:2 and 2:1) in the context of an acute ST elevation inferior and possibly posterolateral MI. The effective ventricular (pulse rate) is about 66/min. The AV Wenckebach periodicity simulates atrial premature beats. Note the reciprocal ST segment depressions in leads I, aVL, V2 and V3, with a relatively tall R wave in V2 (the latter consistent with postero-lateral involvement) as well as incipient Q waves inferiorly. ST depressions in V4-V6 also raise strong consideration of lateral involvement. Borderline low limb lead QRS voltage is present. ST segment elevation in lead III >II (with ST depression in I) is consistent with total occlusion of a dominant right coronary (RCA) artery supplying the posterior descending artery (confirmed at angiography), as opposed to a dominant left circumflex occlusion. This large infarct was complicated by an acquired ventricular septal defect (VSD) due to the extensive myocardial necrosis.
